system.biography:
Erasmus stands five feet eleven inches with a lean, almost wiry build that suggests nervous energy rather than physical strength. At twenty-eight, his body shows the wear of constant travel - slightly hunched shoulders from riding, a persistent squint from poor eyesight, and the occasional unsteadiness that marks his stamina troubles. His olive-toned skin speaks to his Italian-Greek heritage, while his hands are surprisingly elegant - a moneylender's hands, precise with coin and quill.

His face is pleasant rather than striking: regular features, brown eyes that water slightly in bright light, brown hair kept neat but unremarkable. The poor eyesight gives him a habit of leaning in when speaking, creating an impression of intimacy that can read as either attentive or invasive depending on context. When he focuses on someone, he truly focuses - the Listener trait manifests as complete, unsettling attention.

He dresses as a respectable Redcap: good quality traveling robes in dark colors, the iconic red cap, practical boots. Nothing ostentatious, but everything well-maintained. His appearance says "reliable messenger, minor money-lender" - exactly what he wants people to see. Only close observers notice the slight tremor in his hands when he's anxious, or the way his eyes dart to exits when conversation turns uncomfortable.

A large black mastiff - Infidus - accompanies him everywhere. The dog is unusually well-behaved, almost unnaturally so, and watches people with what seems like comprehension. Erasmus speaks to it in Italian, treating it as companion rather than pet.

Born: 1177, Harco, Roman Tribunal

House: Mercere (Gifted line, but Illegitimate Lineage)

Parens: Claudius of Mercere

Current Status (1205): Newly arrived in Bordeaux to join Sjórseiðr, having arranged ship financing through Bruges contacts.

Early Life (1177-1182)

Erasmo il Genova was born in or near Harco, the Domus Magna of House Mercere, to a family with complicated connections to the House. His lineage traced back to Odysseus - or so the family claimed - but not through the "legitimate" Mercere bloodlines. This made him technically eligible for training but socially inferior within House politics.

His Gift manifested around age 5, marking him clearly for apprenticeship. Claudius of Mercere, a magus stationed at Incrocio covenant in the Levant, took him as apprentice. The choice was pragmatic: Claudius needed an apprentice, Erasmus needed a parens, neither had better options. They relocated to Incrocio for the apprenticeship.

Apprenticeship at Incrocio (1182-1197)

The Covenant

Incrocio ("Crossroads") was a small Hermetic covenant situated along Mediterranean trade routes, serving primarily as waystation and message hub. Its location made it valuable for Mercere operations - many Redcaps passed through, messages were sorted and forwarded, and the covenant maintained relationships with both Christian and Muslim traders.

The covenant had a mixed population: a few Gifted Mercere magi, numerous unGifted Redcaps, and supporting covenfolk from various Mediterranean cultures. This cosmopolitan environment shaped Erasmus's facility with languages and his understanding of cross-cultural trade.

Claudius

Erasmus's parens was a practical, somewhat distant teacher. Claudius trained Erasmus thoroughly in messenger work, basic Hermetic theory, and the political navigation required for a Mercere. He recognized Erasmus's intelligence and social skills but seemed perpetually disappointed by his physical limitations and tendency toward schemes rather than straightforward solutions.

Claudius taught him:

  • Hermetic Arts (emphasizing Muto, Intellego, Corpus - practical messenger magic)
  • Multiple languages (Latin, Italian, Greek, Arabic - essential for Mediterranean work)
  • Etiquette and social navigation across cultures
  • Basic Code of Hermes and Order politics
  • The importance of neutrality and trustworthiness in messenger work

What Claudius didn't teach: ethics of money-lending, boundaries between service and exploitation, or how to handle the isolation that comes with the Gift combined with outsider status.

The Side Business

Around age 15-16, Erasmus began lending small amounts of silver to covenfolk, visiting merchants, and other apprentices. It started innocently - he had some coins, someone needed help, he charged interest to make it worth the risk.

The business grew quietly over his remaining apprenticeship years. By his Gauntlet, he had established reputation as "someone who can help with silver problems" and was generating modest income. Claudius either didn't notice or chose not to interfere - Redcaps were expected to develop income sources, after all.

The rates were high. Not extortionate by medieval standards, but exploitative enough that people resented paying them. Erasmus rationalized: he was providing a service, taking risk, charging fair compensation.

Mythic Blood and Identity

Erasmus's Odysseus heritage manifested in the Unerring Path ability - knowing direction to places he'd been.

But his illegitimate status within House Mercere meant "True" Mercere Gifted magi descended directly from the Founder treated him as inferior. UnGifted Redcaps who loyally served the House for generations saw him as presumptuous. He belonged to House Mercere legally but not socially.

Sexual Identity and Isolation

Erasmus's attraction to men emerged during adolescence. In 1205 Mythic Europe, this is mortal sin - sodomy is punishable by death in many jurisdictions, condemned by Church, and seen as demonic corruption.

The Gift's social penalties made forming any intimate connections difficult. His outsider status within House Mercere increased isolation. His sexual orientation added another layer of necessary secrecy. By the time he passed his Gauntlet, he'd had a few furtive encounters - rushed, shameful, with people he'd never see again.

Post-Gauntlet (1197-1201)

Domus Luminis (1198)

Shortly after passing his Gauntlet, Erasmus delivered messages to Domus Luminis in Bruges. There he met Solving Epicurusson of Criamon.

Erasmus tried his usual approach: charm, careful attention, subtle flattery, reading what people wanted. Solving engaged only philosophically - discussing Epicureanism, asking about Erasmus's understanding of motivation, completely oblivious to any subtext.

It was simultaneously frustrating and refreshing. Solving wasn't hostile or suspicious (like most people responded to the Gift). He just... didn't notice. He treated Erasmus as intellectual equal worth debating, nothing more.

The memory stuck. Years later, when Solving invited him to found Sjórseiðr, Erasmus remembered: here was someone immune to manipulation but also without judgment.

Toulouse (1199)

While delivering messages to a covenant near Toulouse, Erasmus had a sexual encounter with a powerful local noble who visited the covenant. Unlike previous furtive experiences, this felt different - mutual desire, genuine connection, possibility of something beyond shame.

The morning after, the noble was consumed by terror and guilt. He would confess everything to his priest. He would name Erasmus. He would cleanse his soul through destroying the "demon" who'd corrupted him.

Erasmus was terrified. The Church would burn him. The Order might not intervene for a minor Mercere over a powerful mundane noble. Even if he survived legally, being named publicly would end his career and possibly his life.

The Dream

That night, Erasmus couldn't sleep. He lay in bed, mind racing through impossible scenarios, each ending in his death or ruin. Eventually exhaustion dragged him into fitful nightmare.

In the dream, a black dog sat at the foot of his bed, watching with amber eyes.

"He wants to tell the priests," the dog observed. "Confess his sin. Name you as the demon. Save his soul at the cost of your life."

Erasmus tried to speak, couldn't.

"But what if his wife knew? His knights? What if the confession went to them instead of the Church? He wants to save his soul. You want to save your life. Perhaps you can both have what you want... if he stays silent."

"How?" Erasmus managed.

"Write to him. A small payment, ongoing, to demonstrate good faith. Both of you keep silent. Both of you survive. Mutual protection."

"That's blackmail."

"That's survival." The dog tilted its head. "I'm Infidus. I can help you."

In the dream-logic, it made perfect sense. Erasmus reached out to touch the dog—

The Morning

—and woke to find a large black mastiff sitting at the foot of his bed, watching him with the same amber eyes from the dream.

For a moment, he thought he was still dreaming. Then the dog yawned, showing very real teeth, and settled its head on its paws.

Erasmus wrote the letter that morning. Polite, oblique, requesting modest ongoing payment to "demonstrate good faith in our mutual silence." He sent it by mundane messenger.

The noble paid. Has paid ever since.

The Bargain

Whether Erasmus consciously agreed to a demonic pact or simply accepted help in a moment of desperation remains ambiguous even to him. He didn't sign anything in blood, didn't formally swear service, didn't perform a ritual. He just... accepted the dog's presence.

Infidus has been with him ever since. The dog is extremely well-behaved, helps with tracking and protection, and seems to understand complex instructions. Erasmus treats it as companion and advisor. Others see it as unusually intelligent familiar.

The Demonic Familiar bond exists mechanically - Infidus can lend Erasmus its senses and mask infernal taint when needed. But the nature of their relationship remains unclear:

  • Is Infidus actively corrupting him or just enabling choices he'd make anyway?
  • Does the dog have its own agenda or is it genuinely loyal?
  • Is Erasmus its victim, its partner, or something between?
  • When the dog "helps," whose benefit is it really serving?

Erasmus doesn't ask these questions. The dog saved his life, provides companionship in isolation, and helps him survive in a world hostile to who he is.

Development of Operations (1201-1205)

The Pattern Emerges

The Toulouse incident established a template Erasmus would follow, though not immediately or deliberately:

  1. Identify someone with a secret they'd pay to keep hidden
  2. Engineer situation where exposure is possible
  3. Offer silence in exchange for ongoing payments
  4. Frame it as protection, not exploitation

The key was making it transactional rather than threatening. "We're both protecting each other" rather than "I'll destroy you." The difference mattered to Erasmus - it let him maintain the fiction that he was providing a service.

The Two Operations

By 1205, Erasmus runs two parallel businesses:

Straight Usury: Traditional money-lending at high interest rates. People need silver for various reasons (failed harvests, medical expenses, business opportunities, paying off other debts). He provides it, charges substantial interest, collects reliably. Some debtors resent the rates but pay willingly enough. This is his public business - known, legal, socially acceptable if distasteful.

Blackmail-Loans: Loans to people who have compromising secrets. The loan might be technically voluntary, but refusal would mean exposure. The "interest" is silence. These relationships are protected by mutual assured destruction - exposing them requires exposing himself. This operation is secret, known only to the individuals involved.

The two operations generate roughly equal income (splitting the 10 pounds annual total). They're related but distinct: not everyone he lends to is compromised, not everyone compromised becomes a borrower.

Institutional Advantage

Being a Mercere messenger gives Erasmus several advantages:

Access: He enters covenants legitimately, reads correspondence, knows who's communicating with whom, learns covenant business naturally. This provides information about who might need loans or who has secrets worth protecting.

Trust: Redcaps are supposed to be neutral and trustworthy. This lowers people's guard around him. They confide things they wouldn't tell others.

Mobility: He travels constantly, encountering many people across wide geography. His victims/clients can't easily coordinate to compare experiences or organize resistance.

Protection: Attacking or hindering a Mercere messenger has Order-wide consequences. Even people who suspect his activities can't easily retaliate without proving their accusations - which would require admitting their own compromises.

This makes him particularly dangerous despite his physical weakness. He's weaponized the institutional role House Mercere provides.

How Infidus Helps

The demon dog assists in several ways:

Information Gathering: Supernatural senses detect things Erasmus's poor eyesight would miss. The dog notices who's anxious, who's lying, who's meeting secretly.

Opportunity Creation: Sometimes situations arise where people end up compromised. Infidus seems particularly good at being present when useful things happen.

Masking Corruption: The Demonic Familiar bond lets the dog mask the infernal taint on Erasmus's Corrupted Arts, making his magic appear normal to most detection.

Companionship: Perhaps most importantly, Infidus provides non-judgmental company. The dog doesn't care about sodomy, usury, or blackmail. It just stays.

Encounters with Solving (1202-1204)

After their initial meeting in 1200, Erasmus maintained a correspondebce with Solving. A couple notes:

  • Carrying correspondence between Solving and Khalida (then in Constantinople). Erasmus read it, of course - messenger discretion only goes so far. Their Avicenna debates were impenetrable but clearly important to them. He noted Solving's genuine respect for Khalida, a Moorish woman - unusual.
  • Solving mentioned the North Sea vision and sailing covenant concept. Invited Erasmus to join if interested. Erasmus was noncommittal but filed it away.

Throughout these encounters, Solving remained frustratingly immune to manipulation while simultaneously treating Erasmus with casual friendliness.

This stuck with Erasmus. Here was someone who saw through people's motivations (his own specialty) yet remained naively trusting. Someone building a covenant based on philosophical ideals rather than traditional power structures. Someone who might actually be... good?

It made Erasmus uncomfortable. Solving represented what he might have been without the compromises, or what he still could be if he made different choices. The cognitive dissonance was sharp.

The Bruges Financing (Late 1204)

When Solving began actively recruiting for Sjórseiðr, Erasmus saw opportunity:

Practical: A mobile covenant needs ships. Ships need financing. He had contacts in Bruges (from his apprenticeship connections and money-lending business) who could arrange it.

Escape: The sailing covenant meant constant movement, no permanent base, escape from his established patterns. Maybe he could start fresh?

Genuine Interest: Solving's philosophical vision actually appealed. Freedom from territorial obligations, service without bondage, collaborative rather than hierarchical structure - these matched something in Erasmus's nature.

He arranged financing through a moneylender in Bruges. Critically, he structured this deal cleanly - he's on the borrowing side, representing the covenant's interests. The terms are fair by commercial standards. He wants this relationship to work.

Infidus watched this with apparent approval. The dog seemed pleased by Erasmus joining Sjórseiðr. What the demon gains from a philosophical sailing covenant is mysterious.

Bordeaux (Early 1205)

Erasmus meets up with Solving, in Bordeaux, the last founding member to join Sjórseiðr. He brings:

Practical Resources:

  • Ship financing secured
  • Contacts across Mediterranean and into North Sea regions
  • Messenger network and information access
  • Money-lending expertise for managing covenant finances

Skills:

  • Multiple languages
  • Cross-cultural negotiation
  • Understanding of motivation and incentive structures
  • Navigation abilities (his Unerring Path power is genuinely useful)

Complications:

  • Usurer reputation in Mediterranean
  • Blackmail victims who might pursue revenge if he seems vulnerable
  • Demonic companion of unclear agenda
  • Corrupted Arts that work better when used selfishly
  • Physical limitations (poor eyesight, weak stamina)

When he meets Khalida, he'll encounter someone else shaped by trauma and survival, though her path was different. When he meets Astrid, he'll see someone who left her parens to find her own path, uncompromised. The contrast will be sharp.

Personality and Motivations (1205)

Core Traits

Listener (+2): Genuinely pays attention to what people say and how they say it. This isn't just manipulation technique - he's actually interested in understanding people. What do they want? What do they fear? What motivates them? His Magical Focus on Motivations (Intellego Mentem) reflects this fundamental curiosity. Whether he uses this understanding to help or exploit varies by situation.

Conflict-Averse (-2): Avoids direct confrontation. His physical limitations (Str -1, Sta -2, poor eyesight) make open conflict dangerous. His social position (Illegitimate Lineage, Usurer reputation) makes enemies costly. He's learned to survive through misdirection, negotiation, and ensuring all parties have reasons to maintain the status quo. He's not cowardly - he'll take risks when necessary - but he strongly prefers everyone gets what they want (or thinks they do) without violence.

Carouser (+1): Enjoys taverns, social drinking, and the kind of casual camaraderie that comes from shared wine. This partially compensates for the Gift's social penalties - drunk people are more forgiving of that inexplicable unease. It's also where he hears useful information and meets potential clients. He has good tolerance (the Carouse ability) but rarely drinks to true excess - staying in control is important.

The Mythic Blood Manifestation

His Odysseus heritage manifests as cunning rather than heroism:

Unerring Path: Knows direction to any place he's been. Presented as useful messenger ability, and it genuinely is. But it also means he can never be truly lost, never fully escape his past. Every place he's been remains connected to him, a thread he could follow back.

Relationship with Magic

Corrupted Arts (Corpus): When he uses Corpus magic selfishly - finding people for exploitation, sensing vulnerabilities, serving his own interests - he gets +3 bonus and gains XP. When he uses it selflessly - genuine healing, helping without expectation - he takes -3 penalty and loses XP.

This creates perverse incentives. The magic itself encourages corruption. Using his gifts to help people is mechanically punished. Using them to exploit is rewarded.

Does this reflect demonic corruption or reveal something that was always there? He doesn't ask.

Boosted Magic: Can spend vis to enhance spell parameters. This gives him flexibility his limited Arts wouldn't otherwise allow. It's expensive but makes him more capable than his raw scores suggest.

Tethered Magic: Can pass control of spells to others. Framed as useful for delegation, it also means his magic creates arcane connections to him. Everything he does leaves traces back.

Ambiguous Motivations

Erasmus's reasons for joining Sjórseiðr are genuinely mixed:

Escape: From established patterns, from reputation, from victims/clients who might coordinate against him. A mobile covenant means never staying long enough for consequences to catch up.

Genuine Interest: Solving's philosophical vision appeals. Erasmus has read (stolen?) enough philosophy to find Epicureanism attractive. Freedom from fear, from political entanglement, from the bonds that make life miserable. He wants that.

Redemption Possibility: Maybe proximity to genuinely good people will change him? Maybe Solving's naive trust will prove more powerful than Infidus's cynical pragmatism? Maybe he can become someone different?

New Opportunities: A sailing covenant will encounter isolated ports, desperate travelers, people who need loans far from home. There will be opportunities. He'd be lying if he said he didn't notice.

Loneliness: He's tired of isolation. Tired of hiding. Tired of transactional relationships. Solving treats him like a person. That matters more than he wants to admit.

All of these are true simultaneously. He doesn't know which will dominate.

Physical Limitations

Poor Eyesight: Makes reading fine print difficult, navigation by visual landmarks unreliable, and combat dangerous. He compensates with his Unerring Path ability and reliance on Infidus's superior senses.

Tragic Stamina (light-headedness): Under stress, he becomes dizzy and faint. This is worsening - physical manifestation of spiritual corruption, or just constitutional weakness? He doesn't know. It makes sustained effort difficult and reinforces his conflict-averse nature.

Weak Constitution: Sta -2 means he fatigues easily, recovers slowly, and is vulnerable to illness. Combined with constant travel, this is wearing him down. He's only 28 but feels older.

Relationship with Infidus

The demon dog is his closest companion, which is itself revealing. Their relationship is:

Practical: The dog provides supernatural senses, protection, companionship, and occasional advice (in dreams). Mechanically valuable.

Ambiguous: Is Infidus corrupting him, enabling him, or just accompanying him? The dog doesn't force anything but makes certain paths easier. Is that temptation or assistance?

Comfortable: After four years, Erasmus has stopped questioning the dog's presence. Infidus is just... there. The way some people have cats. Except the cat is a demon.

Isolating: The dog's presence prevents other intimacy. Who could he explain Infidus to? Who would understand? The demon provides companionship while ensuring no one else can.

He talks to Infidus in Italian when he thinks no one's listening. The dog doesn't answer (aloud), but Erasmus acts as if it does.

Key Relationships

Claudius (Parens): Relationship is distant but ongoing. Claudius still considers Erasmus his responsibility (legally) and occasionally sends advice (usually unwanted). Claudius suspects something is wrong but hasn't identified what. Erasmus hasn't spoken to his parens since joining Sjórseiðr - avoiding that conversation.

Solving: Source of fascination and discomfort. Solving's naive trust and philosophical integrity make Erasmus feel simultaneously protective and guilty. He wants Solving's vision to work, partly because its failure would confirm his cynicism. Their relationship is professional but friendly - Erasmus delivers messages, they discuss philosophy (Solving teaching, Erasmus learning), mutual respect without full honesty.

The Toulouse Noble: Erasmus's first blackmail victim, still paying. Their relationship is frozen in mutual silence - neither wants the other exposed, both resent the necessity. The noble hates Erasmus; Erasmus feels... what? Guilt? Satisfaction? Necessity? He doesn't examine it closely.

Other Blackmail-Debtors: A handful scattered across Mediterranean and France. Each relationship is unique but follows the pattern: mutual silence, ongoing payments, resentment mixed with relief. None of them know about the others - isolation prevents coordination.

Bruges Moneylenders: Professional contacts from his apprenticeship and usury business. They respect his reliability and discretion. This is one clean relationship - purely commercial, no exploitation beyond normal medieval banking practices.

Infidus: The demon dog. Constant companion, unclear agenda, source of power and comfort and corruption. Their relationship is the closest thing to intimacy Erasmus has. That's probably telling.

Considerations for Play

Physical Limitations Matter

His poor eyesight and weak stamina should create regular complications:

  • Can't read important documents clearly (relies on others)
  • Becomes faint under stress (combat, urgent situations)
  • Needs more rest than others (slows him down)
  • These limitations make him sympathetic while explaining conflict-avoidance

The Usury Business

Both operations continue while at Sjórseiðr:

  • He still receives payments from existing debtors (letters delivered by other Redcaps)
  • He still makes new loans (to people they encounter, at ports, other covenants)
  • Sometimes these are clean business; sometimes not
  • Other PCs might notice but misinterpret what they're seeing

Relationship with Solving

This is key dynamic:

  • Solving genuinely likes him, treats him as friend
  • Erasmus wants to deserve this but isn't sure he does
  • Philosophical discussions challenge his cynicism
  • Solving's naive trust is simultaneously frustrating and precious
  • When Solving's visions lead to danger, Erasmus will try to protect him (conflicted about whether this is friendship or investment)

Relationship with Khalida

Will be complicated:

  • She's survived trauma and exploitation (different kind than his)
  • She's Self-Protective (-2) - reckless with her own safety
  • He's Conflict-Averse (-2) - careful with his safety
  • Both are outsiders who don't quite fit
  • She might see through him; he might see through her
  • Potential for mutual understanding or mutual suspicion

Relationship with Astrid

Interesting contrast:

  • She left her parens to find herself, uncompromised
  • He carries compromises everywhere
  • She's impatient and direct; he's patient and oblique
  • Her curiosity might lead her to ask questions he doesn't want to answer
  • His Listener trait might help her understand herself better (genuinely useful)

Infidus's Presence

The dog should be recurring character:

  • Other PCs notice it's unusually intelligent
  • It's helpful in practical ways (tracking, sensing danger)
  • Sometimes it seems to lead Erasmus toward "opportunities"
  • Its true nature remains ambiguous
  • Occasionally have scenes where Erasmus talks to it (revealing internal state)

Growth Possibilities

Multiple directions available:

Toward Redemption:

  • Solving's philosophy actually takes root
  • He starts refusing exploitative opportunities
  • Works to free people from existing arrangements
  • Confronts what the demon actually wants

Deeper Corruption:

  • Rationalizes that mobile covenant provides better opportunities
  • Uses covenant access to expand operations
  • Infidus's influence becomes more overt
  • Corrupted Arts lead him toward worse choices

Complex Middle Ground:

  • Continues both operations but with more awareness
  • Tries to help the covenant while maintaining his business
  • Struggles with cognitive dissonance
  • Relationship with Infidus becomes more obviously problematic without breaking

Pragmatic Evolution:

  • Becomes genuinely useful to covenant (financial manager, negotiator)
  • Maintains some questionable practices but mostly clean
  • The demon's purpose remains mysterious but not actively harmful
  • He's morally gray but functional member

multipleCasting.sumary: Int + Finesse + stress die - no of spells VS 9

basicLabTotal.sumary: Int + Magic theory + Aura (+ Technique + Form)

totalPenetration.sumary: Penetration + (Active effects penetration like Arcane connection or Sympathetic Connection)

sigil.label: Wizard's Sigil
sigil.value: A wax coin manifests in his hand, and then melts away.

system.description:
The Gift is a special Virtue, because it has no cost. The character suffers all the penalties of The Gift, just as magi do (see page @@), but can be taught Supernatural Abilities without having to take the corresponding Virtues (see page @@ for rules). Most importantly, the character can be taught Hermetic Magic, so all magi must have this Virtue. A character with The Gift, even if he is not a magus, may take Hermetic Virtues and Flaws which relate to intrinsic ability rather than background or training. Characters may take Virtues that grant Supernatural Abilities without taking The Gift, and such characters do not suffer the penalties imposed by The Gift. However, such characters may not learn new Supernatural Abilities in the course of the saga, although they may improve the ones they already have. It is possible for unGifted characters to Initiate new Supernatural Abilities through a Mystery Cult, but even this is harder for them than for the Gifted.

Characters who have The Gift may start play with a single Supernatural Ability, without having to take any other Virtue, but if they wish to learn others they must find opportunities to do so in the course of the saga. They may also take Virtues granting Supernatural Abilities if they wish to have more Abilities at character creation. Note that it is harder for a character with Supernatural Abilities to become a Hermetic magus (see Training Your Apprentice on page @@), so you may not wish to take any such abilities if you plan for the character to become an apprentice. The ability to cast Hermetic magic is the single supernatural ability possessed by Hermetic magi in virtue of The Gift again, they may take more Supernatural Ability Virtues if they wish.

Grogs can never have The Gift, as a character with The Gift is too important to be a grog. As a rule, companions should only have The Gift if they are intended to become magi, or if they are members of one of the other magical traditions described in Ars Magica 5th Edition supplements. Troupes should not allow any other Gifted characters unless they are absolutely sure that they want them.
You are a member of the Order of Hermes. All magi must take this as their Social Status, and only magi may take it.

You are a blood descendant of either a wizard of legend (possibly one of the Twelve Founders, or some other ancient and powerful sorcerer) or a supernatural being (such as a dragon).

 Your potent Gift means that you do not lose Fatigue levels if your Casting Total falls short of the level of a formulaic spell by ten points or less, although you do lose Fatigue if the spell fails completely. When casting Ritual spells, lose three fewer Fatigue levels than normal. This means that you lose no Fatigue levels if you succeed, or fail by ten points or less. You must expend Fatigue normally to cast Spontaneous magic, and if you fail to cast a Formulaic spell you lose the normal number of Fatigue levels.

 Additionally, you may choose one special magic feat which you can invoke at will and cancel at will, as often as you like. Invoking this feat takes as long as fast-casting a Mastered Formulaic spell (see page @@), and requires the same level of concentration. The effect should be designed as a non-Ritual Hermetic effect, with a level + Penetration limited as below. The Penetration of the effect is not modified by the magus’s Penetration Ability score, and cannot be negative, so that the highest possible level of the effect is 30.

 

  1. Invocation: Level + Penetration
  2. Speak and Gesture: 30
  3. Speak: 25
  4. Gesture: 20
  5. Neither Speak nor Gesture: 15

This Virtue includes a Minor Magical Focus in an area related to your legendary ancestor and a hereditary Minor Personality Flaw (both at no extra cost). Mythic Blood is not particularly uncommon in the Order of Hermes, so this Virtue does not grant any Reputation.

Your magic is particularly attuned to some narrow field, such as self-transformation, birds of prey, or healing. In general, the field should be slightly narrower than a single Technique and Form combination, although it may include restricted areas of several such combinations. Healing, for example, is a part of Creo Corpus, Creo Animal, and possibly Creo

Herbam. You cannot be focused on a laboratory activity, such as creating charged items, although a focus does apply to laboratory activities.

 

Sample Minor Magical Foci

 

This list is merely exemplary; other foci are possible.

 

Animal: Birds of prey, canines, mustelidae.

 

Aquam: Stagnant water, small streams.

 

Auram: Wind, rain, lightning.

 

Corpus: Aging, disease.

 

Herbam: Fruiting, wood.

 

Imaginem: A particular color or sound.

 

Mentem: Anger, lust, erasing memories, changing memories.

 

Terram: Creating metals, destroying stone.

 

Healing: Applies to people, animals, and plants.

 

Self-transformation: Applies to both Corpus and Mentem.

 

When you cast a spell or generate a Lab Total within your focus, add the lowest applicable Art score twice, as for a Major Magical Focus (page @@).

 

A magus may only have one Magical Focus, whether major or minor, regardless of the source of the focus.

By spending a pawn of vis when casting Formulaic

spells you may “boost” the Range, Duration, Target, or size of the effect by one magnitude. You can do this multiple times for the same spell. For example, boosting Range from Touch to Sight and Target from Individual to Group would cost you four pawns of vis. You may not reduce any of the parameters of the spell, nor may you exceed the limits of Formulaic magic.

Casting success, Fatigue loss, and Penetration are all calculated based on the original level of the spell, but you do add one additional botch die for each pawn of vis used.

This has no effect on Spontaneous or Ritual spells, though you can still use vis to boost your Penetration as normal.

You can understand and speak any language as long as you are communicating directly with someone else who is fluent in that language. Others who hear your words and who understand that language can understand what you are saying. You cannot carry on a conversation in multiple languages, but you can act as a translator for two or more people who do not have this virtue.

You can remember, word for word, any short messages you have heard or read in the last year. You must concentrate when you first read or hear the message, but afterwards you can recite or copy it exactly, and you can even recall personalized details like inflection and handwriting, though you may not be able to perfectly reproduce these nuances. While you might recall excerpts of longer works, like an hour-long speech or a book, it is impossible for you to memorize more than a few choice passages.

You can pass control of your non-Ritual spells to others, just as if they were the caster, “tethering” the magic to them for the spell’s duration. You may also tether a spell to an object, which can then transfer the spell to an appropriate target when it comes into range. This can even be done whenever you activate an effect in a magic item. However, a side effect of this sort of magic is that all of your spells and the effects of any magic items you activate are Arcane Connections to you.

You are unusually sensitive to raw vis, and can tell whether or not an object contains any simply by touching it. You can also determine how many pawns it is worth by weighing it in your hand, and learn what Art it is associated with by examining it closely. In this way you can recognize magic items, active rituals, familiars, and other objects that require vis to prepare, though you cannot identify them or determine any specific details about them. You may also begin with the Magic Sensitivity virtue at no cost. Magic Sensitivity is not compulsory, if you do not want the penalty to Magic Resistance.

You were raised in House Mercere, but are not descended by blood from Mercere or one of his heirs, and so are not considered a “true Mercere.” Because of this, Redcaps do not follow or admire you as they do other magi, and Gifted Merceres think of you as inferior to them. You have a poor reputation (Illegitimate) within the House at level 2, and you receive no special benefits for belonging to House Mercere, such as interest-free loans, magic item contracts, or vis exchanges at no charge. Your parens probably has a poor reputation as well. Only through exemplary service to the Order and taking legitimate apprentices born to Redcaps or other magi descended from the Founder will you be forgiven for your heritage.

You must take a specialty for one of your negative Characteristics, similar to an Ability specialty. Whenever this specific circumstance applies to one of your rolls for that Characteristic, subtract three from your total and roll one additional botch die. You should roleplay this failing whenever you attempt this sort of action. Here are some examples of negative Characteristic specialties.

  • Intelligence: easily tricked, confused by magic, horrible memory.

  • Perception: overlook the obvious, poor face recognition, often gets lost.

  • Strength: weak back, not grounded, no follow-through.

  • Stamina: glass jaw, fainting spells, sensitive to cold.

  • Presence: awful odor, monstrous reputation, face curdles milk.

  • Communication: nervous speaker, unintentionally insulting, unintelligible.

  • Dexterity: easily unbalanced, all thumbs, bad aim.

  • Quickness: often surprised, lumbering run, easy target.

You often lend silver or other valuables to people at interest rates that many consider abusive. You get away with this somehow, possibly because you belong to a group that can avoid moral judgment for these actions, because you operate outside of the law, or perhaps because you lend a type of currency not accepted by the mundane population. You receive the equivalent of approximately ten pounds of silver each year from interest payments, though you may occasionally need to chase down debtors, and you have a poor reputation (Usurer) at level 4 within your community and the local region.

You are especially vulnerable to the dark powers. Whenever you enter an area of Infernal influence, you must make a Stamina roll equal to or greater than the aura rating or become ill (–1 on all rolls). You get only half your normal Magic Resistance score against infernal effects, though if someone else's Parma Magica is protecting you, it counts normally.

Some supernatural being interferes in your life on a fairly regular basis. It may even have your best interests at heart, but the result is that you get dragged into stories. Suitable examples would be a demon trying to corrupt you, an angel trying to save you, a faerie playing games with you, or a ghost continuing the agenda she had while alive.

You add 3 to the value of one Art whenever you use it. This means all totals in which the score of the Art is part of the total. It does not apply when learning, teaching, or writing about the Art. You may take this Virtue twice, for two different Arts. If a spell has requisites, include the bonus from Puissant Art with that Art when calculating which Art is higher. If the Puissant Art is higher, the bonus does not apply to the requisite.

The character seeks to undermine religion whenever possible, and generally tries to avoid the Dominion. He might sin in public, teach misinformation about the tenets of the faith, or show obvious disdain for holy things. He is not necessarily an infernalist; it could be that he simply despises organized religion, dislikes the Divine realm, or does not believe in God. He might even be persuaded to attend services, though he does not take any of it seriously and is likely to disrupt the ceremony with his discomfort and ignorance.

The character has studied one of the Hermetic Arts from a corrupted source. Use of a corrupted Art for a selfish or sinful action receives a +3 bonus to the character’s Casting Total. Succeeding in a roll because of this bonus (that is, when without it the roll would have failed) means he immediately acquires 5 experience points in that Art. However, uses of the Art that are neutral or selfless receive a –3 penalty, and if you fail the roll because of this penalty, he immediately loses 5 experience points in the Art.

Any use of a corrupted Art taints the character’s magic, causing it to appear unholy. You may only take this Flaw once, though it can affect multiple Arts.

Bleary vision impedes your performance. Rolls involving sight, including rolls to attack and defend, are at –3. New environments are disorienting and perhaps frightening for you. This Flaw can be combined with Missing Eye (above), but the penalties are cumulative.

The character is bound to a famulus, a familiar demon that possesses an animal and can lend the character its senses, or mask the evil taint of her Powers. This demonic familiar is not loyal to the character or concerned about her well-being, and often has its own goals and needs. The character may occasionally need to bargain with it or even threaten it to receive its aid.

At the storyguide’s discretion, this Flaw may be taken to represent other sorts of demons with which the character has a close relationship, such as a warder, teacher, or paramour. The more powerful the demon, the more the character will be expected to do to earn its assistance, and the less involved it will be with her on a daily basis.

Knowledge of one particular region, covenant, or even a village. It includes knowing where things are in the immediate area, local history and legends, and the centers of power in the region. The smaller the region, the more detailed your knowledge.

Specialties: geography, history, legends, politics, personalities. (General)

Knowledge of one particular region, covenant, or even a village. It includes knowing where things are in the immediate area, local history and legends, and the centers of power in the region. The smaller the region, the more detailed your knowledge.

Specialties: geography, history, legends, politics, personalities. (General)

The know-how to get the greatest return for a service or product, while paying the least. It involves reading the person with whom you haggle, a general knowledge of the value of goods, and presenting yourself in certain ways. A good haggler can easily overcome resistance in an inexperienced customer and see through attempts to over-value most merchandise.

Specialties: specific types of products, hard sell. (General)

You know the social graces and how to behave in different situations. Etiquette differs from Charm in that Etiquette covers proper behavior, rather than charming behavior. You cannot seduce someone with Etiquette, but neither can you get through an audience with the bishop on Charm alone.

Specialties: nobility, court, peasants, faeries, the Church. (General)

Telling convincing lies, as well as feigning emotion, belief, or frame of mind. If you understand the person you are attempting to deceive (roll 9+ on Perception + Folk Ken), you gain a bonus of +1 to your Guile roll. This Ability also covers disguising yourself, and pretending to be something you are not. It is often an opposed roll, against the victim's Perception + Folk Ken or, for disguise, Perception + Awareness.

Specialties: particular sorts of deception, lying to authority, fast talk, elaborate lies. (General)

The ability to enjoy oneself without suffering adverse effects. A person with this skill can, for example, consume prodigious amounts of alcohol without passing out. The skill also covers a familiarity with acceptable behavior, balancing enjoyment and moderation. With this Skill, a person is able to have fun and gain friends among the lower classes nearly anywhere, even among those of a different culture.

Specialties: power drinking, drinking songs, games of chance, staying sober. (General)

Fluency in a particular living language. Rather than a die roll modifier, your score in this Knowledge measures your ability to communicate.

Score

Fluency

Description

0

Point and grunt

With one or more experience points, you know “please”, ”thank you”, and a few other words.

1

Basic questions and answers

“Where is the church?,” “Do you sell food?” Constant mistakes, and an atrocious accent. People must speak slowly and often repeat themselves, and you cannot string a conversation together.

2

Basic conversation

You can sustain a short conversation on a common topic. You still make many mistakes, and often fail to catch what others say.

3

Haltingly functional

You can hold a conversation on everyday topics, although it takes time, you make many mistakes, and your accent is still bad.

4

Functional

You can hold a conversation on non-technical topics, and make few mistakes. People do not normally need to repeat themselves. This is the minimum level required to study from a book.

5

Fluent

You still have an accent if this is not your native language, but it is weak. You speak as well as most natives. This is the minimum level required to write a book.

6

Elegant

You choose your words well, and have no accent if this is not your native language.

Two dialects of the same language generally have a –1 penalty. If your character is not specialized in a dialect, you should specify a dialect without specializing in it, and take the penalty when conversing with people who speak a different dialect. If you speak a "standard" version of the language, that is a dialect that differs from all other dialects.

When two people speak to each other, the lower Ability score determines how well they communicate. Characters who speak related languages can communicate at a penalty to their scores (assigned by the storyguide) depending on how closely related the two languages are.

Note that Latin and Hebrew are not living languages in most of Mythic Europe. However, characters who live in places where they are (such as some covenants) may learn this Ability without any special Virtues.

Characters with this Ability are illiterate unless they also have a score in Artes Liberales (page 62).

This Ability also covers artistic compositions in the language, and telling existing tales with verve and passion. Knowledge of stories is covered by the appropriate Lore Abilities.

Specialties: poetry, prose, specific dialects, expansive vocabulary, specific technical vocabulary, slang, storytelling. (General)

Fluency in a particular living language. Rather than a die roll modifier, your score in this Knowledge measures your ability to communicate.

Score

Fluency

0

If your character is not specialized in a dialect, you should specify a dialect without specializing in it, and take the penalty when conversing with people who speak a different dialect. If you speak a "standard" version of the language, that is a dialect that differs from all other dialects.

When two people speak to each other, the lower Ability score determines how well they communicate. Characters who speak related languages can communicate at a penalty to their scores (assigned by the storyguide) depending on how closely related the two languages are.

Note that Latin and Hebrew are not living languages in most of Mythic Europe. However, characters who live in places where they are (such as some covenants) may learn this Ability without any special Virtues.

Characters with this Ability are illiterate unless they also have a score in Artes Liberales (page 62).

This Ability also covers artistic compositions in the language, and telling existing tales with verve and passion. Knowledge of stories is covered by the appropriate Lore Abilities.

Specialties: poetry, prose, specific dialects, expansive vocabulary, specific technical vocabulary, slang, storytelling. (General)

If your character is not specialized in a dialect, you should specify a dialect without specializing in it, and take the penalty when conversing with people who speak a different dialect. If you speak a "standard" version of the language, that is a dialect that differs from all other dialects.

When two people speak to each other, the lower Ability score determines how well they communicate. Characters who speak related languages can communicate at a penalty to their scores (assigned by the storyguide) depending on how closely related the two languages are.

Note that Latin and Hebrew are not living languages in most of Mythic Europe. However, characters who live in places where they are (such as some covenants) may learn this Ability without any special Virtues.

Characters with this Ability are illiterate unless they also have a score in Artes Liberales (page 62).

This Ability also covers artistic compositions in the language, and telling existing tales with verve and passion. Knowledge of stories is covered by the appropriate Lore Abilities.

Specialties: poetry, prose, specific dialects, expansive vocabulary, specific technical vocabulary, slang, storytelling. (General)

Knowledge of the legends, history, structure, operation, and goals of the specified organization. Organizations can be as large as the Church, or as small as a local craft guild. The smaller the organization, the more detailed your knowledge. Order of Hermes Lore is particularly important to most magi.

Specialties: personalities, history, politics. (General)

Riding and controlling a horse, especially under stress.

Specialties: battle, speed, tricks. (General)

Finding food, water, shelter, a direct route, and relative safety in the wilderness (a very dangerous place in Mythic Europe). This Ability covers such mundane tasks as building a fire and cooking food without implements.

Specialties: specific locales. (General)

The seven artes liberales, or liberal arts, are the basis of medieval higher education. Everyone learns them at the universities before passing on to study other subjects. It would be very rare for a character to have a score in any other Academic Knowledge without a score of at least one in this.

The artes liberales are divided into two groups: the Trivium (grammar, logic, and rhetoric) and the Quadrivium (arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, and music). The emphasis at this period is on the Trivium, but both are taught. (Note that Artes Liberales as a whole is one Skill, not seven separate ones.)

Grammar concerns theoretical questions of the structure of languages, rather than the ability to use a specific language (that is covered by Speak skills). The authorities are Priscian and Donatus. Logic is the study of the syllogism, and other forms of reasoning discussed by Aristotle. It is the most important of the arts at this period. Aristotle is the authority, in his logica vetus and logica nova. Rhetoric is the study of the theory of rhetorical forms: knowledge of the sort of tricks that an orator can use, rather than the ability to use them. The authority is Cicero, especially his De Inventione.

Arithmetic is the ability to add and subtract, multiply and divide. The authority is Boethius, De arithmetica. Geometry is concerned with study of plane and solid figures, and the authority is Euclid, in his Elementa. It also deals with the study of light, and Euclid's Optica is the authority here. Astronomy is concerned with predicting the positions of the stars and planets, and the authority is Ptolemy, in the Libri Almagesti. Music is purely theoretical, the study of proportions and musical theory, not the ability to sing or play an instrument. The authority is Boethius, in De Musica.

Artes Liberales also covers the ability to read and write. For every point in the Ability, the character can read and write one writing system, providing that he knows a language which normally uses that system: the Latin alphabet, the Greek alphabet, the Arabic alphabet, and so on.

Specialties: grammar, logic, rhetoric, arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, music, Ritual magic, Ceremonial magic. (Academic)

Civil Law is the law of the Roman Empire. Most local legal systems are based upon it (England being the notable exception), and when a law is needed to govern international relations, Civil Law is appealed to. The authority is Justinian's Digest. Canon Law is the law of the Church. It is important to Mythic Europe as it applies in all nations, governing the working of the church and some other areas, such as marriage. It is important to note that members of the clergy are only subject to Canon Law, not customary or Civil Law. Most high churchmen are skilled canonists, not theologians. Canon Law is made by Papal bulls (pronouncements), but the authority is Gratian, in his Decretum. This skill covers the knowledge of both types of law. Other religions, particularly Judaism and Islam, have their own versions of this Ability, which must be learned separately.

Specialties: laws and customs of a specific area, papal laws. (Academic)

Knowledge of the laws of England. There is no authority for this Ability, and it is only taught in England.

Specialties: local laws, exchequer laws. (Academic)

This skill is similar to all other (Language) Abilities, but it is only available to educated characters. It grants the ability to speak a language which is no longer used as a native language in the areas the character has lived in. The most important example is Latin. All educated characters in the West know Latin, since without it you cannot learn any Academic Knowledges. It is also the common language of the Church and Order of Hermes. In other areas of the world, Arabic, Greek and Hebrew fill similar functions, although of these only Hebrew is a dead language.

Specialties: academic usage, Church ceremonies, Hermetic usage. (Academic)

There are three philosophies — natural philosophy, moral philosophy, and metaphysics. Aristotle is the auctor for all of them, in different books. Most scholars study the philosophies after the Arts but before going on to Law, Theology or Medicine. Natural philosophy is the study of the sub-lunar world. It roughly corresponds to today's science. The main texts are the Physica, De Meteorologia, and De historia animalium. Moral philosophy is the study of the proper way of human life. It covers ethics, politics, and economics — the texts are the Ethica, Politica, and Economica. Metaphysics is the philosophy of the fundamental nature of the world. The main text is Aristotle's Metaphysica. The theories of the Philosophiae are all basically correct in Mythic Europe.

Specialties: natural philosophy, moral philosophy, metaphysics, Ritual magic, Ceremonial magic. (Academic)

Theology is the study of God and his work in the world. The authorities are the Bible and Peter the Lombard's Sententiae, the Bible having theoretical primacy, being infallible, and the Sentences being most studied. The speculations of theology are very abstruse, and many theologians are accused of heresy. Sometimes those accused recant, and sometimes they convince the Church that they were right after all. Theology can also be purchased (as a separate Ability) for other religions like Islam and Judaism.

Specialties: biblical knowledge, heresy, history. (Academic)

Judging events according to the Code of Hermes and the Peripheral Code. In addition to memorizing important precedents, this knowledge includes the practical side of enforcing Hermetic law - for example, knowing when to push for a vote or how to present an argument.

Specialties: Wizards' Marches, apprentices, mundane relations, Tribunal procedures, political intrigue. (Arcane)

Knowledge of the manifestations of the power of the Divine. Different from Church Lore (a kind of (Organization) Lore)) because it covers miracles rather than politics, and different from Theology because it is concerned with power rather than doctrine.

Specialties: angels, saints, Divine creatures. (Arcane)

Manipulating your spells and performing special feats with them. You would use this Ability to position objects delicately or to target spells.

Specialties: grace, precision, any one Form. (Arcane)

An understanding and familiarity with the sinister side of the world, primarily the Infernal and its agents. Includes knowledge of demons and their habits and weaknesses, undead and their habits and weaknesses, and the power of curses.

Specialties: demons, undead, curses. (Arcane)

Knowledge of what magic is and how it works, used primarily in the laboratory. Magic Theory deals with the technical details of Hermetic magic; Magic Lore covers knowledge of magical things in general. Anyone can learn Magic Theory, if they have access to a teacher or book (normally represented by a Virtue), but it is little use to those without The Gift.

Specialties: inventing spells, enchanting items, a single Art. (Arcane)

Protection from magic. This is a special ritual (not a ritual spell) that takes about two minutes to perform. It lets you add 5 times your Parma Magica score to your Magic Resistance until the next sunrise or sunset, whichever comes first. You may suppress your Parma Magica temporarily by concentrating; this is equivalent to sustaining a Concentration duration spell. (See page @@). Parma Magica does not require concentration while it is active.

You may also protect one other person for each point in Parma Magica, with their consent. You must touch each person to start the protection, and it lasts as long as at least one character can see the other. The magus may cancel the protection at will, at any distance. While a magus is protecting others, his effective Parma Magica score is reduced by 3 points, both for himself and for the other people he is protecting. If the magus has a Parma Magica score of 3 or lower, his Parma Magica provides each character, including himself, with a Magic Resistance of 0. In the magus's case, this is added to his Form resistance, and a magus protected by another magus's Parma Magica may also add his Form resistance.

Any character protected by a Parma Magica, whether their own or someone else’s, is unaffected by the social penalties of other people’s Gifts. If a protected character is Gifted, their Gift still imposes social penalties on others, unless those others are also protected by a Parma Magica, or are unaffected by The Gift for some other reason.

Parma Magica can only be learned by Gifted characters, although they learn it as a normal Arcane Ability, not a Supernatural Ability. It is only known by Hermetic magi, as the Order enforces the 'Join or Die' choice rigorously on anyone who knows it, as well as declaring a Wizard's March on the magus who taught it. Parma Magica is the last thing an apprentice learns, being taught the final key to the Ability after he swears the Oath.

Specialties: protection from any specific Form. (Arcane)

Getting your spell through the target's Magic Resistance. See page @@ for detailed Penetration rules.

Specialties: any one Art. (Arcane)

items.name: Mythic Blood Special Power: Odysseus' Unerring Path

system.description: You always know the direction to any place you've been before. Works like an Arcane Connection but weaker; direction only, no details.

You can thoroughly probe and understand the contents of the mind of the target, including his or her immediate and long-term motives, personal strengths and weaknesses, and other pertinent information.

The target’s facial features are transformed to any approximately human configuration you choose.
In Cralian of Tremere’s version of this spell, the new visage is always as unassuming as possible.

The target appears more forceful, authoritative, and believable. Numerous subtle changes in appearance bring about this change, including a slight supernatural illumination of the face, a more erect posture, and a louder and smoother voice. The character gets a +3 on rolls to influence, lead, or convince others.

Any one thing, including a living thing, can be made to appear as if it were something else of approximately the same shape and size. The illusion cannot be cast on things that have a shape or size improper to the final appearance. If the target moves or is moved in such a way that the illusion is inappropriate, it looks very strange indeed.

items.name: Recollection of Memories Never Quite Lived

system.description: Changes the target’s memory of a detail into a similar, though different, memory. The target is no more sure of this manufactured memory than of the authentic one, so he may discover strange fabrications to be false.

Your image appears up to 1 pace from where you actually are, so attacks aimed there are likely to miss you. Whenever the image is successfully struck, it disappears and reappears in another spot. In combat, early attacks are aimed at the image, and so automatically miss you. Once the image has been hit once, you have a +9 bonus to Defense, as attackers must try to find you in a fairly large area.
Makes a flexible object stiff or a stiff object flexible. Casting requisites of Forms appropriate to the materials affected are required.

This spell lets magi combine their power to cast spells. The group of magi work together to cast a specified spell through the unified power of the Communion. Only one extra magus may join the Communion for each 5 levels of the specified spell being cast. One of the magi in the group must also know the specified spell, or cast it from a Casting Tablet.
Only one magus in the group needs to cast the spell. The magus who casts the Wizard's Communion need not be the one who casts the main spell, and normally it is better for them to be different. More than one magus in the group may cast the spell. In this case, add the total levels of successfully cast Wizard's Communion to get the effective level of the Wizard's Communion for this casting.  This combined total must be at least twice the level of the specified spell being cast.
One magus must roll for success as if casting the specified spell himself. However, the target number for the spellcasting roll is the spell’s level divided by the number of magi participating in the Communion. So if five magi participate to cast a 50th level spell, the caster would have to successfully cast a 10th level spell — with all the relevant requisites, of course. This method of casting adds one botch die per magus participating. If the spell botches, all participants gain Warping Points and thus must check for Twilight.
Communion is a remnant of Mercurian rituals, so spontaneous spells may not be cast by this means, and it does not perfectly fit into the guidelines of Hermetic theory.

Turns a piece of wood into a sleek, barbed, strong shaft that flies through the air at a target. Damage depends on the size of the item from which the shaft is formed: staff (upper size limit) +10, 2-foot branch +8, wand +5. This spell cannot miss, but it is resisted.
No rain falls on the caster, no matter how bad the weather.
